title: R-Squared (R²): How Well Does Your Model Explain the Data?
concept: R-Squared (R²) scores a model from 0 to 1 by measuring the percentage of variance in the data it explains, compared to a naive baseline that just guesses the average every time.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do we take the square root of MSE to get RMSE?

options.text: To convert the error back into the original, interpretable units
To turn the error into a percentage

exercises.explain: Right — RMSE reports error in the same units as the prediction. Today: a metric that reports a 0-to-1 score instead.

cards.title: Is $50,000 error good or bad?
cards.body: Model A has an RMSE of $50,000 predicting house prices. Model B has an RMSE of $50,000 predicting used bicycle prices. Is $50,000 impressive for both? Not even close — it depends on how much the prices vary in the first place.

cards.title: What is R-Squared?
cards.body: R-Squared (R²) measures the percentage of variance in the actual data that your model explains, compared to a naive baseline that always guesses the average. It's scored roughly from 0 to 1.

cards.title: A report card, not a ruler
cards.body: RMSE is a ruler — it measures distance in real units. R² is more like a report card grade: it tells you what percentage of the "test" (the variation in the data) your model actually explained.

cards.title: R² = 0.85
cards.body: If a house-price model has an R² of 0.85, it explains 85% of the variation in house prices. The remaining 15% is noise or patterns the model doesn't capture.

cards.title: R² can go negative
cards.body: R² = 1 would mean perfect predictions, and R² = 0 means the model is no better than just guessing the average every time. R² can even be negative — that means the model is doing worse than that naive average guess!

cards.title: Naive baseline vs. your model
cards.body: R² compares your model against the simplest possible guess:

left.text:
Always predicts the average
R² = 0 by definition

right.text:
Learns patterns from features
R² closer to 1 means more variance explained

exercises.prompt: What does an R² of 0.70 mean?

options.text: The model explains 70% of the variance in the data compared to the naive average baseline
The model is 70% accurate at guessing exact labels

exercises.explain: R² of 0.70 means the model explains 70% of the variance in the actual data — a much better fit than just guessing the average.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An R² of 0 means the model performs exactly as well as always predicting the average of the data.
exercises.explain: True! R² = 0 is the baseline score for a model that's no better than guessing the average every time.

exercises.sentence: R² compares your model's errors to a naive baseline that always predicts the ___.
exercises.explain: R² measures improvement over a naive baseline that always guesses the average (mean) of the data.

exercises.prompt: A model scores R² = -0.2 on a test set. What does that tell you?

options.text: The model performs worse than simply guessing the average value every time
The model explains -20% variance, which rounds up to a decent score
The R² scale doesn't allow negative numbers, so this is a data error

exercises.explain: A negative R² means the model is actually worse than the naive baseline of always predicting the average — a red flag.

exercises.prompt: Your team is comparing two trained house-price models across different cities. City A has huge price swings ($100k to $2M); City B has similar-priced homes ($300k to $400k). Why might R² be more useful than RMSE for comparing these two models fairly?

options.text: R² is normalized to a 0-1 scale relative to each city's own variance, so it stays comparable even though the two cities have very different price ranges
RMSE is always the same number no matter what city you're in
R² ignores the model's errors completely, so it can't be misleading
RMSE can only be calculated for one city at a time

exercises.explain: Exactly! Because RMSE stays in raw dollars, a $50k RMSE means something very different in a low-variance city than a high-variance one. R² normalizes for that. Next: putting all these metrics side by side.
